content.text: What It Is

Cross-team dependency management is the systematic practice of identifying, tracking, and resolving all commitments that one delivery team must receive from another team in order to complete its own work. In an AI programme, these dependencies span model APIs, data pipelines, infrastructure quotas, security reviews, and regulatory sign-offs — all owned by different teams with independent backlogs and delivery timelines.

A dependency is formalised as a named record in a dependency register, capturing: the requesting team, the providing team, what is needed, when it is needed (the need-by date), when the provider has committed to deliver (the commit date), and the current resolution status (pending, committed, at-risk, blocked, or resolved). Dependencies are distinct from ordinary story-level handoffs; they are cross-team commitments that, if unresolved, will delay a milestone or gate review.
Why It Matters

AI programmes are inherently cross-functional. A single GenAI feature typically touches the data science team (model selection and fine-tuning), the platform engineering team (deployment infrastructure and API gateway), the security team (penetration testing and threat modelling), the data engineering team (feature pipelines), and the product team (UX and integration). Each of those teams has its own sprint backlog and its own capacity constraints.
Prevents milestone slippage caused by invisible blockers:

the most common cause of missed gate reviews is not technical failure but a dependency that was never formally tracked — the model couldn't be deployed because the GPU quota request was never submitted to infrastructure.
Creates accountability across team boundaries:
a named dependency record with a commit date and an owner converts an informal 'we'll try to get to it' into a trackable commitment.
Enables early escalation:
when a dependency turns at-risk (commit date later than need-by date), the programme manager can escalate immediately rather than discovering the problem the week the milestone is due.
Supports capacity planning:

aggregate dependency load on shared platform and security teams reveals whether those teams are over-committed across all programmes — a signal to either hire, prioritise, or defer low-scoring initiatives.
Builds inter-team trust:
teams that consistently receive early, well-specified dependency requests are far more likely to commit and deliver than teams that receive urgent last-minute requests.
Benefits
Reduced milestone risk:
programmes with formal dependency registers report 30–50% fewer milestone slippages attributable to cross-team handoff failures.
Improved resource predictability:
providing teams can capacity-plan against a visible forward dependency load rather than managing a queue of ad hoc requests.
Faster resolution cycles:
when a dependency is well-specified (who needs what, by when, and why), the providing team can act immediately without clarification loops that add days to resolution time.
Visible programme health:
the ratio of resolved to total dependencies is a leading indicator of programme health that appears on programme health dashboards before milestone dates reveal whether the programme is on track.
Reduced blast radius of slippage:
when a dependency slips, its downstream impact on milestone schedules is immediately calculable from the dependency graph rather than discovered after the fact.
Trade-offs and Challenges
Register maintenance overhead:
dependency registers require discipline to keep current. Teams that log dependencies at planning but fail to update resolution status turn the register into a stale artifact that nobody trusts.
Over-specification risk:

treating every cross-team Slack message as a dependency to be logged creates administrative burden that exceeds its value. The register should capture strategic, milestone-blocking dependencies, not every minor handoff.
Accountability resistance:
committing to a named dependency record with a due date requires providing teams to make explicit promises they may not want to make in writing. Cultural change is required alongside the tooling.
Dependency graph complexity:

in large programmes, the dependency graph can grow into dozens of edges. Without visualisation tooling, it becomes unmanageable, and critical-path dependencies are obscured by the noise of minor ones.
Circular dependencies:
it is possible for two teams to each be waiting for something from the other before either can proceed. Detection requires graph traversal, not manual inspection.
How It Works
Dependency management follows a four-phase lifecycle: identification, formalisation, tracking, and resolution.

During identification — typically at sprint planning or programme increment planning — teams explicitly ask: 'What do we need from another team in order to complete our committed work this sprint or this quarter?' Each named need becomes a candidate dependency. The programme manager facilitates a cross-team session where each team walks through its upcoming milestones and declares its dependencies out loud. Other teams then acknowledge or challenge the commit dates.

Formalisation converts a verbal declaration into a dependency record. The standard fields are: dependency ID, requesting team, providing team, description of what is needed, milestone it is blocking, need-by date, commit date (provided by the providing team), resolution status, and escalation owner. The record is created in the dependency register tool and linked to the relevant milestone in the programme timeline.

Tracking happens in two cadences. Daily: teams update their dependency statuses as work progresses — a dependency moves from 'committed' to 'resolved' when the providing team delivers. Weekly: the programme manager reviews all dependencies in 'at-risk' status (commit date is 3 or fewer days beyond need-by date) and 'blocked' status (no commit date, or commit date is more than 3 days beyond need-by date) in the Scrum of Scrums. Blocked dependencies are escalated immediately.

Resolution occurs when the providing team delivers the dependency artefact (an API endpoint, a data schema, a security sign-off document) and the requesting team confirms it is fit for purpose. Only the requesting team can mark a dependency as resolved — this prevents providing teams from self-certifying completion of work the requester hasn't yet validated.
Architecture and Design
The dependency management architecture comprises three components: the dependency register (data store), the dependency visualisation (graph rendering), and the escalation workflow (alert routing).

attrs.alt: Cross-team dependency network showing inter-team commitments between Platform Engineering, Data Science, Product, Security, and MLOps teams

content.text: The dependency register is the source of truth. In its simplest form, it is a spreadsheet or Jira board with a custom issue type. More sophisticated implementations use a dedicated dependency management tool (such as Targetprocess, Planview AgilePlace, or Structure.Gantt for Jira) or a custom database that exposes a REST API. The critical schema element is the explicit link between each dependency and the milestone it is blocking — this makes impact calculation automatic when a dependency slips.

The dependency graph is a directed graph where nodes are teams and edges are dependencies (labelled with the dependency ID and resolution status). The graph is rendered weekly in the programme health dashboard. The programme manager inspects the graph for: critical path dependencies (whose failure would cause the next milestone to slip), clusters of dependencies on a single team (indicating a bottleneck team that is over-committed), and circular dependencies (two teams each waiting on the other).

The escalation workflow triggers automatically when a dependency transitions to 'blocked' status. An alert is routed to the programme director and the two team leads involved. The alert contains the dependency details, the downstream milestone at risk, and the calculated slip impact in days. This ensures that blocked dependencies receive leadership attention within 24 hours rather than being discovered at the next fortnightly programme sync.
When to Use It
Any programme with three or more delivery teams contributing to a shared milestone:
below three teams, dependency tracking can be informal; above three, informal tracking reliably misses commitments.
When a programme has previously experienced milestone slippage caused by cross-team handoff failures:
this is the retrospective trigger that most organisations use to introduce formal dependency management.
During programme increment planning (PI planning) in a SAFe implementation:
dependency identification is an explicit step in the PI planning agenda.
When onboarding a shared platform team that serves multiple programmes:
the platform team needs a visibility tool to balance dependency load across all programmes it serves.
In regulated programmes where cross-team handoffs have compliance implications:
security sign-offs, data privacy reviews, and model risk assessments must be tracked to demonstrate governance.
How to Implement
Establish the dependency register schema:
define the standard fields and create the register in the agreed tool. Publish the schema to all team leads.
Run a dependency identification workshop:
at the start of each programme increment, schedule a 90-minute cross-team session where each team declares its upstream dependencies.
Assign a dependency owner per team:

each team nominates one person (typically the delivery lead or Scrum master) responsible for maintaining their team's dependency records. This person has the authority to escalate on the team's behalf.
Set the review cadence:
weekly dependency review in Scrum of Scrums. Blocked dependencies escalated same-day to the programme director.
Integrate with milestone tracking:
link each dependency record to the milestone it is blocking in the programme timeline tool. Configure automated alerts when a dependency's commit date exceeds its need-by date.
Review and close retrospectively:

at each milestone review, audit the dependency register. Document which dependencies were resolved on time, which slipped, and what the root cause was. Use this data to improve commit date accuracy in the next increment.

dependency_register:
  fields:
    - dep_id: "DEP-001"
      requesting_team: "data-science"
      providing_team: "platform-engineering"
      description: "Model serving API endpoint for inference — v2 schema required"
      blocking_milestone: "GATE-3-pilot-done"
      need_by: "2025-10-01"
      commit_date: "2025-09-26"
      status: "committed"   # pending | committed | at_risk | blocked | resolved
      escalation_owner: "programme-director"
      notes: "Platform confirmed dedicated GPU node provisioned week of Sep 22"

    - dep_id: "DEP-002"
      requesting_team: "data-science"
      providing_team: "data-engineering"
      description: "Feature pipeline delivering PII-redacted contract text to model"
      blocking_milestone: "GATE-3-pilot-done"
      need_by: "2025-09-20"
      commit_date: "2025-10-05"
      status: "at_risk"
      escalation_owner: "programme-director"
      notes: "15-day gap. Data Eng estimating PII redaction library procurement delay."

    - dep_id: "DEP-003"
      requesting_team: "platform-engineering"
      providing_team: "security"
      description: "Penetration test sign-off for model serving endpoint"
      blocking_milestone: "GATE-4-production-ready"
      need_by: "2025-11-01"
      commit_date: "2025-10-28"
      status: "committed"
      escalation_owner: "ciso"

escalation_rules:
  at_risk_threshold_days: 3   # commit_date - need_by > 3 → at_risk
  blocked_threshold_days: 7   # no commit or gap > 7 → blocked
  blocked_response_sla_hours: 24
  notification_channel: "slack:#programme-alerts"
Examples

# Dependency health check script
# Run weekly before Scrum of Scrums to surface at-risk and blocked deps

from datetime import date, timedelta
import json

def check_dependency_health(deps: list[dict]) -> dict:
    today = date.today()
    at_risk = []
    blocked = []
    resolved = []
    pending_commit = []

    for dep in deps:
        if dep["status"] == "resolved":
            resolved.append(dep)
            continue

        if not dep.get("commit_date"):
            blocked.append({**dep, "reason": "No commit date provided"})
            continue

        need_by = date.fromisoformat(dep["need_by"])
        commit = date.fromisoformat(dep["commit_date"])
        gap = (commit - need_by).days

        if gap > 7:
            blocked.append({**dep, "gap_days": gap})
        elif gap > 3:
            at_risk.append({**dep, "gap_days": gap})
        else:
            pending_commit.append(dep)

    return {
        "summary": {
            "total": len(deps),
            "resolved": len(resolved),
            "at_risk": len(at_risk),
            "blocked": len(blocked),
            "on_track": len(pending_commit),
        },
        "at_risk": at_risk,
        "blocked": blocked,
    }

# Usage
deps = json.load(open("dependency_register.json"))
report = check_dependency_health(deps)
print(f"Blocked: {report['summary']['blocked']} | At Risk: {report['summary']['at_risk']}")
for dep in report["blocked"]:
    print(f"  BLOCKED: {dep['dep_id']} — {dep['description']} (blocking {dep['blocking_milestone']})")
Real-World Example

A retail bank running a GenAI loan underwriting programme with six contributing teams had experienced three consecutive quarterly milestone slippages. A post-mortem on the third slippage revealed that all three were caused by the same pattern: the data engineering team had verbally committed to delivering feature pipelines but had never formally recorded a commit date, and the programme manager had assumed the commitment was solid until two weeks before the milestone when the data engineering team revealed they were two sprints behind.

The programme introduced a dependency register in week one of the next programme increment. In the first dependency identification workshop, 23 cross-team dependencies were recorded for the quarter. Of these, 8 were immediately flagged at-risk because the providing teams could not commit to the requested need-by dates. These 8 were escalated to the programme director within 24 hours. Five of the eight were resolved by resequencing providing team work; two were resolved by temporarily borrowing a contractor from another programme; and one caused the programme to defer a non-critical integration feature by one sprint.

The milestone was delivered on time. The programme manager attributed the improvement entirely to the visibility created by the dependency register — the problems were the same as in previous quarters; the difference was that they were identified and resolved six weeks earlier.
Common Mistakes
Treating the dependency register as a status reporting artifact rather than an active management tool:
registers that are updated before weekly meetings but ignored between them fail to surface emerging risks in time.
Allowing providing teams to set unrealistic commit dates to avoid conflict:

commit dates should be negotiated, not dictated. If a providing team cannot commit to a need-by date, that must be surfaced immediately rather than absorbed into a commit date the team knows it will miss.
Not linking dependencies to milestones:
standalone dependency records with no milestone link cannot be used to calculate downstream impact when a dependency slips.
Marking dependencies resolved prematurely:
only the requesting team should mark a dependency resolved — providing teams self-certifying creates a false sense of progress.
Failing to close the retrospective loop:
dependency registers that are never audited post-milestone miss the opportunity to improve commit date accuracy and escalation speed for the next increment.
Best Practices
Raise dependencies as early as possible:
a dependency raised six weeks before its need-by date gives the providing team realistic options; one raised six days before creates a crisis.
Specify dependencies precisely:
'need the API' is not a dependency specification. 'Need the model serving API endpoint, v2 schema with < 200ms p99 latency, deployed to staging environment, by September 26' is.
Treat at-risk dependencies with the same urgency as blocked ones:
at-risk is a leading indicator of blocked; programmes that only respond to blocked dependencies consistently discover problems too late.
Visualise the dependency graph weekly:
text registers miss structural problems (bottleneck teams, circular dependencies) that are immediately visible in a graph view.
Celebrate resolved dependencies in team retrospectives:
dependency resolution is collaborative cross-team work; recognising it publicly reinforces the behaviour.
Protect providing team capacity:
shared platform and security teams that are dependency providers for multiple programmes need explicit capacity ring-fencing. Without it, they will always be the bottleneck.
Performance and Scalability

A dependency register scales well up to approximately 60 active dependencies before the volume of at-risk and blocked items in weekly reviews becomes unmanageable for a single programme manager. At this scale, introduce tiered escalation: team leads handle all at-risk dependencies within their domain; only blocked dependencies with cross-domain impact reach the programme director.

At the enterprise level (multiple programmes sharing platform teams), a dependency aggregation view across all programmes becomes essential. Platform teams need a consolidated view of all dependency commitments they have made across programmes to prevent over-commitment. This is typically implemented as a portfolio-level dependency dashboard that aggregates from individual programme registers, showing platform team load by week.
Tooling and Ecosystem

Common tooling patterns: Jira with a custom 'Dependency' issue type and a linked 'Blocks' relationship field; SAFe's PI board (physical or digital) with explicit dependency wires; Targetprocess or Planview for portfolio-level visibility; Notion or Confluence databases for lightweight programme-level tracking. The key integration requirement is a bidirectional link between the dependency record and both the requesting team's sprint board and the programme timeline — without this integration, dependency status updates and milestone impact calculations are always out of sync.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Strengths
Weaknesses
Best For
Dependency register with structured fields
Auditable; enables impact calculation; scales
Requires discipline to maintain
Programmes > 3 teams
Informal Slack/email tracking
Low overhead; fast
Not auditable; misses dependencies; no escalation
Small 2-team projects
SAFe PI board dependency wires
Visual; cross-team; built into ceremony
Requires SAFe adoption; physical board doesn't integrate with tools
SAFe organisations
Risk register inclusion
Consolidated risk view
Dependencies lost in noise of other risks
Compliance-heavy programmes
Gantt chart with dependencies
Timeline visibility
Doesn't capture ownership or resolution status
Waterfall projects
Security and Reliability

Dependency records may reference sensitive information: model architectures, data schema details, or security assessment timelines. The register should be access-controlled at the programme level, visible to all contributing teams but not to external vendors unless explicitly required. For dependencies involving the security team, dependency records should not contain the security assessment findings — only the delivery timeline commitment. Full security assessment results are stored in the security team's own system of record.

Reliability of the dependency management process depends on consistent execution of the weekly review cadence. Programmes that cancel or abbreviate their Scrum of Scrums during busy delivery periods lose dependency visibility exactly when they need it most. Treat the weekly dependency review as a fixed, inviolable ceremony.
Mental Model

Cross-team dependency management is like air traffic control for a busy airport. Each aircraft (delivery team) has a planned route (milestone plan) that requires specific clearances from the control tower (providing teams) — runway access, altitude clearances, weather updates. The dependency register is the control tower's display board: every aircraft, its planned route, what clearances it needs, and which have been granted. When a clearance is denied or delayed (a dependency turns at-risk or blocked), the controller reroutes immediately rather than waiting until the aircraft is on final approach with nowhere to go. The key insight is that air traffic control is not about preventing aircraft from flying — it is about giving every aircraft the information it needs to fly its planned route safely, and about surfacing conflicts early enough to resolve them.
Key Takeaways
Cross-team dependency management converts informal verbal commitments into named, tracked records with explicit commit dates, statuses, and escalation paths.
The dependency register schema must include a milestone link — without it, the impact of a dependency slip on the programme schedule cannot be calculated.
At-risk dependencies (commit date within 3 days of need-by) deserve the same escalation urgency as blocked ones — they are blocked in slow motion.
Only the requesting team can mark a dependency as resolved — provider self-certification creates false progress signals.
Weekly dependency reviews in the Scrum of Scrums, with same-day escalation of blocked items, is the minimum viable cadence for programmes with three or more contributing teams.
Providing teams that serve multiple programmes need a consolidated dependency load view to prevent over-commitment across the portfolio.
